items.question: Which mechanism describes missingness that depends only on observed variables, not on the hidden missing value?
items.explanation: Missing At Random (MAR) means the probability of missing depends on observed data—e.g. gender predicts survey skip rate—but not on the unobserved income value itself.

items.question: A column has 2% MCAR missing values and 50,000 rows. What is often the simplest defensible first step?
items.explanation: With low MCAR missingness and ample rows, complete-case analysis loses little data and avoids imputation assumptions.

items.question: Why should imputation happen before StandardScaler in a numeric pipeline?
options: Scalers require finite values to compute mean and variance
Test sets never have missing values
items.answer: Scalers require finite values to compute mean and variance
items.explanation: StandardScaler needs defined moments; imputing first yields a complete training matrix for both imputer and scaler statistics fit on train.

items.question: High earners are less likely to report income. Which mechanism is most plausible?
items.explanation: Missingness is tied to the unobserved income value—classic Missing Not At Random—so naive mean imputation can systematically understate income.

items.question: Which approach lets a gradient-boosted tree use missing values without a single fill value?
items.explanation: XGBoost and LightGBM learn default directions for NaN during splits, treating missingness as a first-class pattern rather than a replaced constant.
